source: Mit l脙陇chelndem Gesichte schaute Siddhartha dem hinweggehenden M脙露nche nach. Der Schlaf hatte ihn sehr gest脙陇rkt, sehr aber qu脙陇lte ihn der Hunger, denn er hatte nun zwei Tage nichts gegessen, und lange war die Zeit vor脙录ber, da er hart gegen den Hunger gewesen war. Mit Kummer, und doch auch mit Lachen, gedachte er jener Zeit. Damals, so erinnerte er sich, hatte er sich vor Kamala dreier Dinge ger脙录hmt, hatte drei edle und un脙录berwindliche K脙录nste gekonnt: Fasten 芒聙?Warten 芒聙?Denken. Dies war sein Besitz gewesen, seine Macht und Kraft, sein fester Stab, in den flei脙聼igen, m脙录hseligen Jahren seiner Jugend hatte er diese drei K脙录nste gelernt, nichts anderes. Und nun hatten sie ihn verlassen, keine von ihnen war mehr sein, nicht Fasten, nicht Warten, nicht Denken. Um das Elendeste hatte er sie hingegeben, um das Verg脙陇nglichste, um SinnenIust, um Wohlleben, um Reichtum! Seltsam war es ihm in der Tat ergangen. Und jetzt, so schien es, jetzt war er wirklich ein Kindermensch geworden.
translation_a: With a smiling face, Siddhartha watched the departing monk. Sleep had greatly strengthened him, but hunger tormented him greatly, for he had not eaten for two days, and the time was long gone when he had been hard against hunger. With sorrow, and yet also with laughter, he thought of that time. At that time, he recalled, he had boasted three things to Kamala, had mastered three noble and insurmountable arts: Fasting - Waiting - Thinking. This had been his possession, his might and strength, his steady staff, in the busy, toilsome years of his youth he had learned these three arts, nothing else. And now they had left him, none of them was his anymore, not fasting, not waiting, not thinking. He had given her up for what was most miserable, for what was most perishable, for sensual pleasure, for a good life, for riches! It had indeed been strange for him. And now, it seemed, he really had become a childish man.
translation_b: With a smiling face, Siddhartha peered after the departing monk. Sleep had greatly strengthened him, but hunger now greatly tormented him, for he had not eaten in two days, and long past was the time when he had been hard toward hunger. With grief, and yet with laughter too, he thought back to that time. He recalled that he had boasted of three things to Kamala; he had been master of three noble and invincible arts: fasting芒聙聰waiting芒聙聰thinking. That had been his property, his might and strength, his solid staff. In the zealous, arduous years of his youth he had learned those three arts, and nothing else. And now they had abandoned him; none of them were his anymore: not fasting, not waiting, not thinking. He had given them away for the most wretched things, the most ephemeral things, for sensuality, for luxury, for wealth! His life had indeed been bizarre. And now, so it seemed, he had truly become a child person.
comment: The first translation is too literal and doesn't quite show understanding of the original ("had become a childish man" - that is not what the original intended to say, the second translation gets this better). The second translation uses better words overall and also better expressions - "His life had indeed been bizarre" is a more accurate and also much better-sounding sentence than "It had indeed been strange for him." The second translation features a better mastery of language and a better representation of the original.

source: Pl脙露tzlich zuckte Hans Castorp ge脙陇rgert und beleidigt zusammen. Eine T脙录r war zugefallen, es war die T脙录r links vorn, die gleich in die Halle f脙录hrte, 芒聙?jemand hatte sie zufallen lassen oder gar hinter sich ins Schlo脙聼 geworfen, und das war ein Ger脙陇usch, das Hans Castorp auf den Tod nicht leiden konnte, das er von jeher geha脙聼t hatte. Vielleicht beruhte dieser Ha脙聼 auf Erziehung, vielleicht auf angeborener Idiosynkrasie, 芒聙?genug, er verabscheute das T脙录renwerfen und h脙陇tte jeden schlagen k脙露nnen, der es sich vor seinen Ohren zuschulden kommen lie脙聼. In diesem Fall war die T脙录r obendrein mit kleinen Glasscheiben gef脙录llt, und das verst脙陇rkte den Chok: es war ein Schmettern und Klirren. Pfui, dachte Hans Castorp w脙录tend, was ist denn das f脙录r eine verdammte Schlamperei! Da 脙录brigens in demselben Augenblick die N脙陇hterin das Wort an ihn richtete, so hatte er keine Zeit, festzustellen, wer der Misset脙陇ter gewesen sei. Doch standen Falten zwischen seinen blonden Brauen, und sein Gesicht war peinlich verzerrt, w脙陇hrend er der N脙陇hterin antwortete.
translation_a: Suddenly Hans Castorp winced, annoyed and offended. A door had slammed shut, it was the front left door that led straight into the hall - someone had let it shut or even slammed it behind them, and that was a noise that Hans Castorp couldn't stand, that he had always hated. Perhaps that hatred was from education, perhaps from innate idiosyncrasy芒聙聰enough he detested door-smashing, and could have punched anyone who threw it in front of his ears. In this case the door was also filled with small panes of glass, and that added to the shock: it was a smash and clink. Pfui, thought Hans Castorp angrily, what kind of damned sloppiness is that! Incidentally, since the seamstress spoke to him at the same moment, he had no time to establish who the culprit was. But there were lines between his blond brows and his face was awkwardly contorted as he answered the seamstress.
translation_b: Suddenly Hans Castorp flinched芒聙聰he was annoyed and offended. A door, the one to his left that led to the lobby, had banged shut芒聙聰someone had simply let it slam, or perhaps even slammed it intentionally, and that was a noise that Hans Castorp absolutely could not tolerate, he had always hated it. Perhaps it was a learned dislike, perhaps an inborn idiosyncrasy芒聙?whatever it was, he abhorred banging doors and could have slapped anyone guilty of slamming one within earshot. In this case, the door was divided into little glass panes, which only heightened the shock: it was a bang and a rattle. 芒聙聹Damn it,芒聙?Hans Castorp thought angrily, 芒聙聹what kind of sloppiness is that?芒聙?Since the seamstress had said something to him at the same moment, he had no chance to determine who the malefactor was. But as he answered the seamstress, there were deep furrows between his blond eyebrows and his face was wrenched with distress.
comment: The first translation uses less good sentence structure, some parts sound a bit awkward. It also for some reason still contains the German word "Pfui" instead of its translation. The second translation sounds much smoother, it uses better sentence structure and also better words and expressions (for example "within earshot" vs. "in front of his ears". While the first translation tends to be too literal in places, the second focuses on the meaning and sounds more natural and sophisticated.

source: Im Restaurant war es hell, elegant und gem脙录tlich. Es lag gleich rechts an der Halle, den Konversationsr脙陇umen gegen脙录ber, und wurde, wie Joachim erkl脙陇rte, haupts脙陇chlich von neu angekommenen, au脙聼er der Zeit speisenden G脙陇sten, und von solchen, die Besuch hatten, benutzt. Aber auch Geburtstage und bevorstehende Abreisen wurden dort festlich begangen, sowie g脙录nstige Ergebnisse von Generaluntersuchungen. Manchmal gehe es hoch her im Restaurant, sagte Joachim; auch Champagner werde serviert. Jetzt sa脙聼 niemand als eine einzelne etwa drei脙聼igj脙陇hrige Dame darin, die in einem Buche las, aber dabei vor sich hin summte und mit dem Mittelfinger der linken Hand immerfort leicht auf das Tischtuch klopfte. Als die jungen Leute sich niedergelassen hatten, wechselte sie den Platz, um ihnen den R脙录cken zuzuwenden. Sie sei menschenscheu, erkl脙陇rte Joachim leise, und esse immer mit einem Buche im Restaurant. Man wollte wissen, da脙聼 sie schon als ganz junges M脙陇dchen in Lungensanatorien eingetreten sei und seitdem nicht mehr in der Welt gelebt habe.
translation_a: The restaurant was well lit, elegant, and comfortable. It was to the right of the lobby, directly across from the social rooms, and was used, as Joachim explained, primarily for new arrivals or residents who either had missed a regularly scheduled meal or had visitors. But birthdays or imminent departures were celebrated there, too, as were favorable results of a general checkup. Things could get very lively in the restaurant on occasion, Joachim said; they even served champagne. There was no one there now except one lady, perhaps thirty years old, sitting alone and reading芒聙聰humming to herself the whole time while drumming softly on the tabletop with the middle finger of her left hand. When the young gentlemen had seated themselves, she changed places, so that her back was to them now. She was standoffish, Joachim explained in a low voice, and always ate in the restaurant with just her book. Rumor had it that she had entered a tuberculosis sanatorium as a very young girl and had never lived in the outside world since.
translation_b: The restaurant was bright, elegant and comfortable. It was just to the right of the hall, opposite the conversation rooms, and, as Joachim explained, was mainly used by newly arrived guests dining out of time, and by those who had visitors. But birthdays and forthcoming departures were also celebrated there, as well as favorable results from general medical examinations. Sometimes things get really busy in the restaurant, said Joachim; champagne is also served. Now there was no one in it but a single lady of about thirty, reading a book while humming to herself and tapping the tablecloth lightly with the middle finger of her left hand. When the young people were settled, she changed places to have her back to them. She was shy of people, Joachim explained quietly, and always ate with a book in the restaurant. They wanted to know that she had entered a pulmonary sanatorium as a very young girl and hadn't lived in the world since.
comment: The second translation contains some mistakes, such as the wording "They wanted to know" instead of "Rumor had it" and the disregard of indirect language in Joachim's first sentence. The first translation uses much better language and captures the style of the original well. It also used better expression whereas the second translation tends to stick too strong to literal wording (for example the first translation uses "who either had missed a regularly scheduled meal" where as the second uses "dining out of time". The first translation reads smoothly, the second one a bit awkward.

source: Er bat Herrn Doktor Fuchstaler, die Ambulanz zu leiten, und mu脙聼te sich gestehen, da脙聼 er lieber hier geblieben als auf den Galitzinberg gefahren w脙陇re. Und doch, es mu脙聼te sein. Nicht nur sich allein gegen脙录ber war er verpflichtet, der Sache weiter nachzugehen; noch allerlei anderes gab es heute zu erledigen. Und so entschlo脙聼 er sich f脙录r alle F脙陇lle, Herrn Doktor Fuchstaler auch mit der Abendvisite zu betrauen. Das junge M脙陇dchen mit dem verd脙陇chtigen Spitzenkatarrh dort im letzten Bett l脙陇chelte ihm zu. Es war dieselbe, die neulich bei Gelegenheit einer Untersuchung ihre Br脙录ste so zutraulich an seine Wange gepre脙聼t hatte. Fridolin erwiderte ihren Blick ungn脙陇dig und wandte sich stirnrunzelnd ab. Eine wie die andere, dachte er mit Bitterkeit, und Albertine ist wie sie alle 芒聙?sie ist die Schlimmste von allen. Ich werde mich von ihr trennen. Es kann nie wieder gut werden.
translation_a: He asked Doctor Fuchstaler to run the ambulance and had to admit that he would rather have stayed here than gone to the Galitzinberg. And yet it had to be. He was under an obligation not only to himself to pursue the matter further; there were a lot of other things to do today. And so he decided, just in case, to entrust Doctor Fuchstaler with the evening rounds as well. The young girl with the suspicious catarrh there in the last bed smiled at him. It was the same one who had pressed her breasts so trustingly against his cheek the other day during an examination. Fridolin returned her look ungraciously and turned away frowning. One like the other, he thought bitterly, and Albertine is like them all - she is the worst of them all. I will break up with her. It can never be good again.
translation_b: He asked Dr Fuchstaler to take charge of the rounds, though he had to admit that he would rather have stayed on than drive out to the Galitzinberg. And yet it had to be done. He did not just owe it to himself to pursue the matter further; and there were many things still to be attended to today. And so, just in case, he decided to entrust Dr Fuchstaler with the evening rounds as well. The young girl with suspected acute bronchitis over in the last bed smiled at him. She was the one who during a recent examination had taken the opportunity to press her breast so intimately against his cheek. Fridolin returned her gaze ungraciously and turned away with a frown. They芒聙聶re all the same, he thought bitterly, and Albertine no different from the rest 芒聙?in fact she芒聙聶s the worst of them all. We芒聙聶ll have to part. Things can never be the same again between us.
comment: The wording of the second translaton is much better and smoother. It also fits the original style and time period of the book better, for example when using "We'll have to part" instead of the too modern "I will break up with her" in the first translation.

source: Pangloss r脙漏pondit en ces termes: 脗芦 O mon cher Candide! vous avez connu Paquette, cette jolie suivante de notre auguste baronne; j芒聙聶ai go脙禄t脙漏 dans ses bras les d脙漏lices du paradis, qui ont produit ces tourments d芒聙聶enfer dont vous me voyez d脙漏vor脙漏; elle en 脙漏tait infect脙漏e, elle en est peut-脙陋tre morte. Paquette tenait ce pr脙漏sent d芒聙聶un cordelier tr脙篓s savant, qui avait remont脙漏 脙 la source; car il l芒聙聶avait eue d芒聙聶une vieille comtesse, qui l芒聙聶avait re脙搂ue d芒聙聶un capitaine de cavalerie, qui la devait 脙 une marquise, qui la tenait d芒聙聶un page, qui l芒聙聶avait re脙搂ue d芒聙聶un j脙漏suite, qui, 脙漏tant novice, l芒聙聶avait eue en droite ligne d芒聙聶un des compagnons de Christophe Colomb. Pour moi, je ne la donnerai 脙 personne, car je me meurs.
translation_a: Pangloss replied as follows: 芒聙聰My dear Candide! you knew Paquette, that pretty maidservant to our august Baroness. In her arms I tasted the delights of paradise, which directly caused these torments of hell, from which I am now suffering. She was infected with the disease, and has perhaps died of it. Paquette received this present from an erudite Franciscan, who took the pains to trace it back to its source; for he had it from an elderly countess, who picked it up from a captain of cavalry, who acquired it from a marquise, who caught it from a page, who had received it from a Jesuit, who during his novitiate got it directly from one of the companions of Christopher Columbus.15 As for me, I shall not give it to anyone, for I am a dying man.
translation_b: Pangloss replied in these terms: 芒聙聹O my dear Candide! you knew Paquette, that pretty servant of our august baroness; I tasted in his arms the delights of paradise, which produced those torments of hell with which you see me devoured; she was infected with it, she may have died of it. Paquette got this present from a very learned Cordelier, who had gone back to the source; for he had had it from an old countess, who had received it from a cavalry captain, who owed it to a marquise, who had received it from a page, who had received it from a Jesuit, who , being a novice, had had her in direct line with one of the companions of Christopher Columbus. As for me, I won't give it to anyone, because I'm dying.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other translation is pretty bad. It's very literal. Also, it contains many errors and inconsistencies. For example, Paquette is clearly a woman. In the BAD translation, she is referred to with the subject pronoun "she" but then the masculine possessive "his" is used when talking about her arms.

source: Ils se pla脙搂aient dans la salle basse d芒聙聶un cabaret, qui avait 脙 sa porte des filets noirs suspendus. Ils mangeaient de la friture d芒聙聶脙漏perlans, de la cr脙篓me et des cerises. Ils se couchaient sur l芒聙聶herbe ; ils s芒聙聶embrassaient 脙 l芒聙聶脙漏cart sous les peupliers ; et ils auraient voulu, comme deux Robinsons, vivre perp脙漏tuellement dans ce petit endroit, qui leur semblait, en leur b脙漏atitude, le plus magnifique de la terre. Ce n芒聙聶脙漏tait pas la premi脙篓re fois qu芒聙聶ils apercevaient des arbres, du ciel bleu, du gazon, qu芒聙聶ils entendaient l芒聙聶eau couler et la brise soufflant dans le feuillage ; mais ils n芒聙聶avaient sans doute jamais admir脙漏 tout cela, comme si la nature n芒聙聶existait pas auparavant, ou qu芒聙聶elle n芒聙聶e脙禄t commenc脙漏 脙 脙陋tre belle que depuis l芒聙聶assouvissance de leurs d脙漏sirs.
translation_a: They placed themselves in the lower room of a cabaret, which had black nets hanging from its door. They ate fried smelts, cream and cherries. They lay down on the grass; they kissed apart under the poplars; and they would have liked, like two Robinsons, to live perpetually in this little place, which seemed to them, in their bliss, the most magnificent on earth. It was not the first time they had seen trees, blue skies, grass, heard the water running and the breeze blowing through the foliage; but they had doubtless never admired all that, as if nature had not existed before, or had only begun to be beautiful since the satisfaction of their desires.
translation_b: They would sit in the low-ceilinged room of a tavern with black nets hanging at its door. They would eat fried smelts, cream, and cherries. They would lie down in the grass; they would go off and kiss under the poplars; and they would wish that, like two Robinson Crusoes, they could live forever in that little spot, which seemed to them, in their bliss, the most magnificent on earth. It was not the first time they had seen trees, blue sky, or lawn, or that they had heard water trickling and the breeze blowing through the leaves; but they had never before admired all of this, it was as if nature had not existed before, or as if it had begun to be beautiful only once they had slaked their desires.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other translation is too literal and contains a few errors. For example, the bad translation has the wrong tense on its verbs. The French tense used throughout the text is the Imperfect, used here to describe a habitual action in the past. All the actions mentioned are actions that the characters did a number of times over a certain period of time. In English, it is appropriate to translate this idea with "they used to" or "they would +V-infinitive". Ex: Ils mangeaient = They used to eat / They would eat. // The bad translation chose to use the preterit (i.e., they ate) throughout the text, which makes it sounds like each action only happened once.

source: Le jour revint, et il fut bien constat脙漏 que, sous l芒聙聶influence d芒聙聶un courant sous-marin, le banc de glace d脙漏rivait vers le nord avec rapidit脙漏. Cette masse flottante emportait le Forward, clou脙漏 au milieu de l芒聙聶ice-field, dont on ne voyait pas la limite ; dans la pr脙漏vision d芒聙聶une catastrophe, dans le cas o脙鹿 le brick serait jet脙漏 sur une c脙麓te ou 脙漏cras脙漏 par la pression des glaces, Hatteras fit monter sur le pont une grande quantit脙漏 de provisions, les effets de campement, les v脙陋tements et les couvertures de l芒聙聶脙漏quipage ; 脙 l芒聙聶exemple de ce que fit le capitaine MacClure dans une circonstance semblable, il fit entourer le b脙垄timent d芒聙聶une ceinture de hamacs gonfl脙漏s d芒聙聶air de mani脙篓re 脙 le pr脙漏munir contre les grosses avaries ; bient脙麓t la glace, s芒聙聶accumulant sous l芒聙聶influence d芒聙聶une temp脙漏rature de sept degr脙漏s (-14脗掳 centigrades) ; le navire fut entour脙漏 d芒聙聶une muraille de laquelle sa m脙垄ture sortait seule.
translation_a: The day returned, and it was clearly observed that, under the influence of an undercurrent, the ice bank was drifting rapidly towards the north. This floating mass carried the Forward, nailed to the middle of the ice-field, the limit of which could not be seen; in anticipation of a catastrophe, in case the brig should be thrown on a slope or crushed by the pressure of the ice, Hatteras brought on deck a large quantity of provisions, the camping effects, the clothes and blankets of the crew ; Following the example of what Captain MacClure did in a similar circumstance, he had the vessel surrounded by a belt of hammocks inflated with air so as to protect it against serious damage; soon the ice, accumulating under the influence of a temperature of seven degrees (-14脗掳 centigrade); the ship was surrounded by a wall from which its mast alone emerged.
translation_b: Day broke, and it was observed that the ice-field was floating north, in a strong submarine current. This floating mass transported the Forward, nailed to the heart of the field, without visible limit; foreseeing catastrophe if the brig was dashed on the coast or crushed by the ice, a large quantity of provisions, camping equipment, and blankets for the crew were brought on deck; like Captain McClure in similar circumstances, Hatteras had the ship protected with a ring of hammocks inflated with air, to prevent the worst damage; with soon a temperature of seven (芒聢?4脗掳C) and a build-up of ice, the ship was surrounded by a high wall, only its mast emerging.
comment: This was a difficult decision. For some reason, I am not a big fan of either translation. Both include great as well as poor phrasing. Ultimately, the reason why I chose the second translation is because the first one contains an error. In French, "[...] Hatteras fit monter sur le pont une grande quantit脙漏 de provisions [...]" means that Hatteras HAD SOMEONE do something. He didn't do this thing himself. The translation I selected uses an avoidance strategy: "[...] a large quantity of provisions [...] were brought on deck" where the use of the passive erases agentivity (the person or thing performing the action of the verb). That works, because even though it is not a very accurate translation, it does not contradict the original meaning. The translation I did NOT select, however, did go into this contradiction with: "[...] Hatteras brought on deck a large quantity of provisions [...]". This sentence is a contradiction to the original text and it is false. It might seem like a detail, but it could potentially be of crucial importance for the story. You never know.

source: La vie du bord devint l芒聙聶objet de s脙漏rieuses m脙漏ditations ; Hatteras la r脙漏gla avec le plus grand soin, et le r脙篓glement fut affich脙漏 dans la salle commune. Les hommes se levaient 脙 six heures du matin ; les hamacs 脙漏taient expos脙漏s 脙 l芒聙聶air trois fois par semaine ; le plancher des deux chambres fut frott脙漏 chaque matin avec du sable chaud ; le th脙漏 br脙禄lant figurait 脙 chaque repas, et la nourriture variait autant que possible suivant les jours de la semaine ; elle se composait de pain, de farine, de gras de b脜聯uf et de raisins secs pour les puddings, de sucre, de cacao, de th脙漏, de riz, de jus de citron, de viande conserv脙漏e, de b脜聯uf et de porc sal脙漏, de choux, et de l脙漏gumes au vinaigre ; la cuisine 脙漏tait situ脙漏e en dehors des salles communes ; on se privait ainsi de sa chaleur ; mais la cuisson des aliments est une source constante d芒聙聶脙漏vaporation et d芒聙聶humidit脙漏.
translation_a: Life on board became the subject of serious consideration; Hatteras regulated it with the greatest attention, the guidelines being posted in the common room. The men got up at six; the hammocks were aired three times a week; the floors of both rooms were scrubbed with hot sand each morning; boiling tea accompanied each meal; the food changed as much as possible, depending on the day of the week: sugar, cocoa, tea, rice, lemon juice, preserved meat, beef, salt pork, cabbage, and pickled vegetables; plus puddings made from bread, flour, beef suet, and raisins; the kitchen was situated outside the common rooms; its heat was lost, but cooking is a constant source of evaporation and hence humidity.
translation_b: Life on board became the object of serious meditation; Hatteras settled it with the greatest care, and the rules were posted in the common room. The men got up at six o'clock in the morning; the hammocks were exposed to the air three times a week; the floor of the two rooms was scrubbed every morning with hot sand; hot tea figured at every meal, and the food varied as much as possible according to the days of the week; it consisted of bread, flour, beef fat and raisins for puddings, sugar, cocoa, tea, rice, lemon juice, preserved meat, salted beef and pork, cabbage, and pickled vegetables; the kitchen was located outside the common rooms; they thus deprived themselves of their warmth; but cooking food is a constant source of evaporation and moisture.
comment: The translation I selected is much more idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other one is too literal and contains mistranslations. For example, the French verb "r脙漏gler" (line 1) has multiple meanings (to regulate, to settle, to adjust, to tune, etc.) The bad translation picked one of the meanings that is obviously not appropriate for the context. It picked the meaning of "settle" while the good translation picked the correct meaning of "regulate".

source: Le soir, pourtant, l芒聙聶abb脙漏 Mouret se sentit trop las. Il remit son d脙漏part au lendemain. Le lendemain, il se donna un nouveau pr脙漏texte : il ne pouvait abandonner sa s脜聯ur ainsi seule avec la Teuse ; il laisserait une lettre pour qu芒聙聶on la conduis脙庐t chez l芒聙聶oncle Pascal. Pendant trois jours, il se promit d芒聙聶脙漏crire cette lettre ; la feuille de papier, la plume et l芒聙聶encre 脙漏taient pr脙陋tes, sur la table, dans sa chambre. Et, le troisi脙篓me jour, il s芒聙聶en alla, sans 脙漏crire la lettre. Tout d芒聙聶un coup, il avait pris son chapeau, il 脙漏tait parti pour le Paradou, par b脙陋tise, obs脙漏d脙漏, se r脙漏signant, allant l脙 comme 脙 une corv脙漏e qu芒聙聶il ne savait de quelle fa脙搂on 脙漏viter. L芒聙聶image d芒聙聶Albine s芒聙聶脙漏tait encore effac脙漏e ; il ne la voyait plus, il ob脙漏issait 脙 d芒聙聶anciennes volont脙漏s, mortes en lui 脙 cette heure, mais dont la pouss脙漏e persistait dans le grand silence de son 脙陋tre.
translation_a: However, when evening came, Abb脙漏 Mouret felt too weary. He put off his departure to the next day. On the next day, he found another pretext: he could not leave his sister like that, on her own with La Teuse; he would leave a letter, asking for her to be taken to Uncle Pascal. For three days he promised himself he would write that letter; the sheet of paper, the pen and ink were ready on the table in his bedroom. And on the third day he went off without writing the letter. He had suddenly taken his hat and set off for the Paradou, out of stupidity, just obsessed and resigning himself to it, going there as if for some chore he could find no way of avoiding. The image of Albine had grown even fainter; he could not see her any more, he was acting in obedience to old resolves, now dead for him, but whose impetus still persisted in the great silence of his being.
translation_b: In the evening, however, Father Mouret felt too tired. He postponed his departure until the following day. The next day he gave himself a new pretext: he could not leave his sister thus alone with La Teuse; he would leave a letter to be taken to Uncle Pascal. For three days he promised himself to write this letter; the sheet of paper, the pen and the ink were ready, on the table, in his room. And on the third day he went away without writing the letter. All of a sudden, he had taken his hat, he had left for Paradou, out of stupidity, obsessed, resigning himself, going there like a chore he didn't know how to avoid. Albine's image had faded again; he no longer saw her, he was obeying old wills, dead within him at that moment, but whose thrust persisted in the great silence of his being.
comment: The translation I selected is better because it is much more idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. Also, the other one contains errors/mistranslations. For example, in the French sentence [...] il laisserait une lettre pour qu芒聙聶on la conduis脙庐t chez l芒聙聶oncle Pascal" the object pronoun "la" (="her" or "it" depending on whether the object is animate or inanimate) obviously refers to the sister mentioned earlier. The GOOD translation has this pronoun correctly referring to the sister: [...] he would leave a letter, asking for her to be taken to Uncle Pascal" where "her" is the sister. However, the BAD translation misunderstood the "la" and translated it as if it referred to the letter itself "[...] he would leave a letter to be taken to Uncle Pascal."

source: Cependant, le 19, Simpson parvint 脙 s芒聙聶emparer de l芒聙聶un d芒聙聶eux 脙 quatre cents yards du navire ; il avait eu la pr脙漏caution de boucher son trou de refuge, de sorte que l芒聙聶animal fut 脙 la merci des chasseurs. Il se d脙漏battit longtemps, et, apr脙篓s avoir essuy脙漏 plusieurs coups de feu, il finit par 脙陋tre assomm脙漏. Il mesurait neuf pieds de long ; sa t脙陋te de bull-dog, les seize dents de ses m脙垄choires, ses grandes nageoires pectorales en forme d芒聙聶ailerons, sa queue petite et munie d芒聙聶une autre paire de nageoires, en faisaient un magnifique sp脙漏cimen de la famille des chiens de mer. Le docteur, voulant conserver sa t脙陋te pour sa collection d芒聙聶histoire naturelle, et sa peau pour les besoins 脙 venir, fit pr脙漏parer l芒聙聶une et l芒聙聶autre par un moyen rapide et peu co脙禄teux. Il plongea le corps de l芒聙聶animal dans le trou 脙 feu, et des milliers de petites crevettes enlev脙篓rent les moindres parcelles de chair ; au bout d芒聙聶une demi journ脙漏e, le travail 脙漏tait accompli, et le plus adroit de l芒聙聶honorable corporation des tanneurs de Liverpool n芒聙聶e脙禄t pas mieux r脙漏ussi.
translation_a: However, on the 19th Simpson succeeded in seizing one of them four hundred yards from the ship; he had taken the precaution of plugging his hole of refuge, so that the animal was at the mercy of the hunters. He struggled for a long time, and after having suffered several shots, he was finally knocked out. It was nine feet long; its bull-dog head, the sixteen teeth of its jaws, its large pectoral fins in the shape of fins, its small tail provided with another pair of fins, made it a magnificent specimen of the family of sea dogs. The doctor, wanting to keep his head for his collection of natural history, and his skin for future needs, had both prepared quickly and inexpensively. He plunged the body of the animal into the fire pit, and thousands of small shrimps removed the smallest bits of flesh; at the end of half a day the work was accomplished, and the most skilful of the honorable corporation of tanners of Liverpool could not have succeeded better.
translation_b: Still, on the 19th, Simpson succeeded in getting one four hundred yards distant from the ship; he had taken the precaution to close its hole in the ice, so that it could not escape from its pursuers. He fought for a long time, and died only after receiving many bullets. He was nine feet long; his bull-dog head, the sixteen teeth in his jaw, his large pectoral fins shaped like little wings, his little tail with another pair of fins, made him an excellent specimen. The doctor wished to preserve his head for his collection of natural history, and his skin for future contingences, hence he prepared both by a rapid and economical process. He plunged the body in the hole, and thousands of little prawns removed the flesh in small pieces; at the end of half a day the work was half finished, and the most skilful of the honorable corporation of tanners at Liverpool could not have done better.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is much more idiomatic and sounds like it could be the original text. The bad translation sticks to the French too much, and does not flow in English.

translation_a: But it turned out that you are just a dignitary, ignorant and stupid, like all the dignitaries who were before you and will be after you. And, like all dignitaries, you are a tyrant. Only tyranny can explain the hurricane you raised around a piece of mediocre sausage, or the fact that you brought an unknown young man from the street to your place. And, perhaps, out of the same tyranny, you brought Volodya Makarov closer to you, about whom I know only one thing, that he is a football player. You are a bard. You need jesters and freeloaders. I have no doubt that that Volodya Makarov ran away from you, unable to endure bullying. Like me, you must have systematically turned him into a fool.
translation_b: 芒聙聹But it turned out that you芒聙聶re simply a functionary, ignorant and dull, like all functionaries who were before you and will be after you. And like all functionaries you芒聙聶re a two-bit tyrant. Only by two-bit tyranny can the hurricane which you raised around a piece of mediocre sausage or the fact that you brought home an unknown young man off the street be explained. And perhaps out of the same two-bit tyranny you brought near to yourself Volodya Makarov, about whom I know only one thing, that he is a soccer player. You are a lord. You need jesters and hangers-on. I don芒聙聶t doubt that this Volodya Makarov ran away from you, not having endured the derision. It must be that just like me, you were systematically turning him into a fool.
comment: Both translations are almost equally perfect but the second one is still better. 1) "lord" from the second translation is the exact translation of the Russian "脨卤脨掳脩聙脨赂脨陆" while "bard" from the first translation doesn't convey a necessary meaning 2) According to the source text the last sentence implies the meaning of continuous action as it is important for the story. The author of the second translation is right to use Past Continuous

translation_a: As a soccer player Volodya represented a complete opposite to Getzke. Volodya was a professional sportsman芒聙聯the other was a professional player. Important to Volodya was the general course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome芒聙聯Getzke strove only to show his own skill. He was an old, experienced player, not intending to maintain the honor of the team: he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sort of sports organization because he had compromised himself by moves from club to club for money. They forbade him to participate in matches for the play-off of a championship. They invited him only for friendly games, demonstration matches and trips to other countries. Skill united in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He was suspicious of the players芒聙聯both those with whom he played and the opponents. He knew that he would drive in the balls on any team. The rest was not important to him. He was a hack.
translation_b: As a football player, Volodya was the exact opposite of Getska. Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player. Volodya was interested in the overall course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome - Getsuke sought only to show his art. He was an old, experienced player who did not intend to uphold the honor of the team; he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sports organization, because he compromised himself by moving from club to club for money. He was banned from participating in championship matches. He was invited only to a friendly game, exhibition matches and trips to other countries. Art was combined in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents. He knew that he would score goals for any team. The rest didn't matter to him. He was a hack.
comment: There is no doubt that the first translation is better than the second one for the following reasons: 1) There is a mistake in spelling the name of the character in the second translation. It should be "Getzke" while the author of the second translation writes "Getska" or even "Getsuke". 2) According to the source text a story is told about two characters. The second sentence of the passage should give information about two different people while there is only one person mentioned in the second sentence of the 2nd translation: "Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player" 3) The author of the second translation uses a word-by-word translation of the sentence "He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents" which cannot be used here
